Integrated spatial and multi-omic analysis of ovarian cancer to uncover immune evasion and therapeutic targets

Background

Methods

2. tCycIF spatial protein-guided collection of GeoMx Regions of interest (ROIs) for spatial transcriptomics

1. Ovarian cancer samples

Aims

~50% of EOCs contain tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes, linked to better survival.
PD-1/PD-L1 blockade shows limited benefit due to weak biomarkers.
Broad TME profiling is crucial for better patient stratification.

Results

Aleksandra Shabanova 1, Iga Niemiec 1,  Zhihan Liang 1, Saundarya Shah 1, Ada Junquera 1, Elias Ruuska 1, Ziqi Kang 1, Gayani Anandagoda 1,  Matilda Salko 1, Anni Virtanen 1,3, Ulla-Maija Haltia 1, 2, Anniina Färkkilä 1,2,3,4

1 Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland 
2 Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Department of Oncology, Clinical Trials Unit, Comprehensive Cancer Center, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland 
3 Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic Center, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland. 
4 Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute for Life Sciences, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland

4. Enriched cell interactions motivate multi-modal integration of immune ecosystems

Spatial multi-omic exploration of immune microenvironment

sity of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland

4. Enriched cell interactions motivate multi-modal integration of immune ecosystems

Spatial multi-omic exploration of immune microenvironment
Discovery of spatially-restricted immune escape mechanisms 
Elucidation of underlying molecular mechanisms
Discovery of new potential therapeutic targets

Conclusions

Spatial analysis revealed close myeloid–T cell interactions and diverse immune infiltration patterns, highlighting the complexity of the tumor immune ecosystem. Next, t-CyCIF and GeoMx data will be integrated within a network framework to uncover immune regulatory mechanisms and identify novel biomarkers.

Speaker notes:
About 50% of epithelial ovarian cancer patients exhibit intraepithelial tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs). Their presence correlates with prolonged survival, suggesting an immunoreactive subset of patients.
Immune checkpoint blockade (e.g., PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors) relies on preexisting TILs. However, clinical trials show limited efficacy, partly due to the lack of reliable biomarkers to identify responders.
A single biomarker is insufficient to predict benefit in ovarian cancer. The tumor microenvironment (TME)—including immune cell distribution, subsets, function, and interactions—plays a critical role in regulating anti-tumor immunity.

n ovarian cancer. The tumor microenvironment (TME)—including immune cell distribution, subsets, function, and interactions—plays a critical role in regulating anti-tumor immunity.
Comprehensive evaluation of the TME is therefore essential for improving patient selection and therapeutic outcomes.

That is why our aims are: Spatial multi-omic exploration of immune microenvironment
Discovery of spatially-restricted immune escape mechanisms 
Elucidation of underlying molecular mechanisms
Discovery of new potential therapeutic targets
 For this we are using a unique cohort of 16 paired samples, where we had both pre-chemo and post-chemotherapy sample subjected for multi-omic profiling. We also collected 34 unpaired chemo-treated samples. 

Collcted FFPE samplea are then cut to two slides: First adjacnet is used for multiplex immunofluorescence analysis with tCycIF. Processed images are then used for tenattaive Region of Interest ROI selection.
Second adjacent slide is used for spatial transcriptomics – GeoMx, which scan is first ovarlayed with tCycIF to select ROIs, then segmneted seperately for tumor and stroma areas. And these segmnets are then used for whole transcriptome analysis.

For this projct, we created a semi-automatised ROI selection tool.
The tool has 3 key steps:
It preprocesses the selected channels to identify the immune hubs.

scriptome analysis.

For this projct, we created a semi-automatised ROI selection tool.
The tool has 3 key steps:
It preprocesses the selected channels to identify the immune hubs. Here, as examples, we have CD4 and CD8 channels, where the final mask image shows poteantial immune hubs of corresponding cell type.
The tool identifies the overlapping hubs between selected channels and proposes candidate ROIs based on the size
Finally, human is required to inspect candidate ROIs and select final tentative ROIs

In total, we are using following intregrated spatial protein and transcriptomic profilimg for 5 immune cells. Studing their interactions in stroma and tumor.

We performed preliminary analysis of 14 tCycIF samples. The images were segmneted and cell phenotyyped to receive final singke cell table with their spatial coordinates and phenotype. This dataset is then used in SPACEStat package to study spatial distribution on singkle-cell level. Firstly, by qunatifying global distances, as median distance between two cell pairs with relfected stength of their spatial closeness calculated as AUC of Index of nearest neighbour measurement collected vs distance measured, we showed strong proximity of cells of same type, and next T cell and Myeloids. Next, we turned to the tumor–stroma interface — the critical frontline where tumors and immune cells meet.

wed strong proximity of cells of same type, and next T cell and Myeloids. Next, we turned to the tumor–stroma interface — the critical frontline where tumors and immune cells meet. We identified tumor-stromal interface cells as a cell has at least one neighbor from the other group within 35 um.

Then, we calculated both average distance of each cell type to the TSI, as well as the tumor cluster density with DBSCAN (lower density mean …). Between sample variability is evidently striking here. Across samples, the tumor–stroma interface distance can range widely — from as little as 50 microns to as much as 150. Yet within a single sample, distances remain relatively stable, with the largest gap between two immune populations reaching about 80 to 160 microns.
What’s surprising is that these distances don’t align with tumor cluster density. Even samples with very long interface distances can show tumor densities similar to those with much shorter distances. This suggests that factors beyond simple tumor density are shaping immune positioning at the tumor–stroma boundary.

Similarly, when we Assign each immune cell a regional label ("tumor" or "stroma") based on its local neighborhood composition within 50 um: An immune cell is classified as **"tumor"** region only if it has **at least 20% more tumor neighbors than stroma neighbors**.

on its local neighborhood composition within 50 um: An immune cell is classified as **"tumor"** region only if it has **at least 20% more tumor neighbors than stroma neighbors**.

 Based on this context of immune cells, we calculate then The density of immune cells relative to the size of each region and finally the immune infiltrartion - immune_density_tumor / immune_density_stroma


taking a look at myellid cells (CD11c and Macrophages) and T cells we can see highly sample dependent patterns independnet of tumor architecture .

Finally, We defined enrichment as the fold change between the observed number of interactions and the average number from ten rounds of random shuffling. A fold change greater than one means enrichment, equal to one means no change, and less than one indicates depletion.
We performed this analysis for two, three and four immune cell types with increasing radii, from 30 microns to 120 with step of 45 microns.

Such analysis revealed myeloid and t cell attraction in half of samples, compared to second half, but interestingly it didn’t capture triplets and quadroplets -> fold change of ~0.5

These results highlight how diverse and complex the tumor immune microenvironment is across patients, and they underscore the importance of deeper, multi-modal analysis of both spatial features (on whole sample level) and patient data.

or immune microenvironment is across patients, and they underscore the importance of deeper, multi-modal analysis of both spatial features (on whole sample level) and patient data. That is why our next steps would be the immune ecosystems as dynamic, interconnected network of interactions and integrate it with mini-bulk GeoMx
